title: Easy-Build Lemonade Stand
summary: A kid-height lemonade stand from one 1x12 and five fence pickets, with a bolt-off sign board so the whole thing breaks down flat and fits in the car.
description:
The best thing about this design isn't the box — it's that the sign legs attach with screws instead of nails, on purpose, so the tall part comes off. A lemonade stand gets used four Saturdays a year and stored the other forty-eight, and a stand that breaks down flat is a stand that actually survives to next summer instead of being left in the rain. (The source builder discovered the other reason the hard way: with the sign attached, it doesn't fit in the car.)

Construction is as basic as woodworking gets: two 1x12 shelves boxed in with fence pickets, more pickets across the front as cladding, and two 1x3 furring strips carrying the sign up top. Every cut is a straight crosscut. If this is a kid's first build — and a lemonade stand is a good candidate for exactly that — they can do real work on it: marking cuts, driving screws, sanding, and absolutely the painting.

Dog-eared pickets are fine here (the cheap ones with the clipped corners) — the dog ears can face down inside the box or get cut off, and the front cladding hides most of them anyway. Sand everything; picket splinters and small hands are a bad mix. Save the picket offcuts for small menu signs — the angled sign board leaves scraps that want to become 'LEMONADE 50¢.'

tools.note: Every cut is a straight crosscut. A miter saw or even a handsaw works.
Screws over nails for the sign legs — that's what makes it removable.
Pickets splinter and kids' hands are the customer. Sand at least the counter and front.

materials.name: Cedar fence picket, 5-1/2" x 72"
materials.note: Dog-eared pickets are fine — the cheap ones. Ends, front cladding, and sign.

materials.name: Exterior screws, 1-1/2", self-tapping, star drive
materials.note: Or 1-1/2" brads and glue for the cladding — but keep SCREWS on the sign legs so it stays removable.

cutList.note: Two per end, boxing the shelves into an I-shape.
Across the front between the ends. Twelve total picket cuts at 23-1/2" — cut them all in one batch.
Cut to fit across the legs. Save the offcuts for mini menu signs.

steps.title: Box the shelves
steps.body:
Stand the two 1x12s parallel — one as the counter, one as the lower shelf — and attach two picket boards across each end to form the box. Screws, glue and brads, or even a staple gun all work; two fasteners per shelf per board keeps everything from racking.

Set the lower shelf a few inches off the ground so it clears wet grass.

steps.title: Clad the front
steps.body: Run the remaining 23-1/2" pickets vertically across the front, edges touching, fastened top and bottom into the counter and shelf. The cladding is what makes it read as a stand instead of a shelf unit — and it gives you the billboard for painting.

steps.title: Screw on the sign legs
steps.body:
Attach the two 1x3 legs to the outside of the 1x12 ends with screws — screws specifically, not nails or glue, because these come OFF for storage and transport. Three screws per leg, into the counter and shelf edges.

Mark which leg goes on which end (a pencil letter inside works) so reassembly lines the same holes up every time.

steps.title: Add the sign and paint
steps.body:
Cut the sign picket to fit and screw it across the tops of the legs. Angled slightly is a legitimate choice — the source build did it on purpose and it reads as friendly, not crooked. Cut small signs from the offcuts if the crew wants a menu.

Sand everything a hand can touch, then hand the paint over. The paint job is not your department. Price of lemonade is also not your department.

images.alt: Painted wooden lemonade stand with tall sign board and picket front
